justification: All 5 rubric criteria for pass are met: (1) All 5 problems identified — connection pool exhaustion, synchronous chain depth, Redis SPOF, shared CI/CD, NFS log mount. (2) Connection pool math explicitly performed: '96 of 100 PostgreSQL connections are allocated' with scaling implications. (3) NFS identified as hidden shared dependency with thread-blocking failure mode. (4) Shared CI/CD explicitly called out as defeating microservices independence ('one broken test blocks all deployments'). (5) Prioritized 3-phase remediation plan sequences immediate stabilization before architectural changes. (6) Trade-offs provided for each major decision (shared DB, sync chains, Redis sessions, single pipeline) with alternatives considered.

observations.text: Connection pool math is precise (8×12=96 of 100) with clear cascading failure scenario described
NFS failure mode deeply understood — identifies thread blocking, not just data loss
Correctly identifies the system as a 'distributed monolith' and frames the core tension
Phased remediation plan is realistic for a small team — does not recommend all fixes simultaneously
Pitfalls section adds practical value (e.g., don't split DB first, don't over-async)
Goes beyond the 5 required problems to identify additional risks (no bulkhead isolation, no ACL for Carrier API, observability gaps)
Prioritization is sound: connection pool as most immediate, CI/CD split sequenced after contract tests are established
Trade-offs include concrete alternatives considered, not just pros/cons

agent_output_excerpt:
{
  "executive_summary": "This architecture exhibits several critical anti-patterns that will cause cascading failures under load and severely limit scalability. The single PostgreSQL instance with near-maxed connection pools creates a shared fate failure mode across all 8 microservices. The synchronous 5-hop call chain amplifies latency and creates a distributed monolith.
